agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is being scoped where customers would see their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands zero location sharing due to driver retention problems. This is a fundamental business policy conflict that cannot be resolved at the engineering level — it requires executive arbitration.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Last-Mile Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"The driver or delivery person physically transporting a package to the customer"},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery"},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to a specific customer's delivery address"},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The precision level at which a courier's position is shared — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to zone-level approximation (e.g., '2 stops away')"},{"term":"Active delivery","definition":"The state in which a courier has picked up a package and is in transit to the delivery destination"}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (traffic, break) and exact GPS is shared","expected_behavior":"Customer sees courier 'stuck' at a location — raises support tickets, increases anxiety. This is a known anti-pattern in real-time tracking that exact-GPS amplifies."},{"scenario":"Courier is making multiple deliveries on same route","expected_behavior":"Customer sees courier 'going the wrong way' or passing near their address without stopping — generates complaints. Exact GPS without route context causes more harm than good."},{"scenario":"Courier delivers to unsafe area and customer shares GPS with third party","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS creates a physical safety risk for the courier — this is a real liability concern in the logistics industry, not hypothetical."},{"scenario":"Courier ends shift but location data continues to stream","expected_behavior":"Must ensure tracking is strictly scoped to active delivery window only — stale or post-delivery location sharing is a privacy violation."},{"scenario":"Customer uses exact GPS to intercept courier mid-route","expected_behavior":"Known industry problem — customers walking/driving to meet couriers disrupts route optimization and creates safety issues."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Both sides carry financial risk. Losing driver retention increases recruiting and training costs (typically $3K-$5K per driver in logistics). Losing customer transparency may reduce NPS and increase churn. The financial analysis is incomplete without data on actual driver attrition rates vs. customer satisfaction lift from tracking.","operational":"Driver retention is an existential operational concern — a courier network with high churn cannot deliver reliably. Customer-facing tracking is a 'nice to have' that becomes meaningless if there are no couriers to track. Operations has the stronger operational argument.","user":"Customers benefit from delivery visibility but industry data shows zone-level tracking ('your courier is 3 stops away') achieves nearly the same satisfaction as exact GPS. Exact coordinates often cause MORE anxiety (watching a dot not move). Couriers are also users of this system — their experience and safety are first-class concerns.","risk":"CRITICAL: Exact GPS sharing creates potential physical safety liability for couriers. If a courier is harmed by someone using real-time GPS data shared through this feature, the company faces legal exposure. GDPR and similar privacy regulations may also impose constraints on sharing worker location data with third parties. Neither team has mentioned legal/compliance review, which is a major gap.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"SEVERE — Exact GPS sharing directly affects their sense of safety, autonomy, and dignity. Operations reports this is already causing attrition. Their physical safety is a non-negotiable concern that outweighs customer convenience."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"MODERATE — Want delivery visibility, but industry benchmarks show approximate tracking (ETA-based or zone-based) satisfies 85-90% of the transparency need without exact GPS."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"HIGH — Their KPIs around transparency and NPS are at stake, but their requirement as stated (exact GPS at all times) is the maximalist position, not the only way to achieve their goal."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"HIGH — Driver retention directly affects their ability to fulfill deliveries. Their requirement as stated (zero visibility) is also a maximalist position."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED — This is a major gap. Worker location data sharing has regulatory implications under GDPR, CCPA, and labor law. Must be involved before any decision."},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate — this is a strategic business decision, not a technical or domain one."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because this is an unresolved contradiction between two business stakeholders with legitimate but opposing concerns. No engineering analysis or domain expertise can resolve a policy conflict — this requires executive decision-making. Additionally, legal/compliance has not been consulted on what is even permissible, which could invalidate both positions. The spec as given is unimplementable.","high_confidence_areas":["The two requirements are in direct, irreconcilable contradiction as stated","Exact GPS sharing creates real safety and privacy risks for couriers — this is well-established in the logistics industry","Legal/compliance review is mandatory before any decision","Zone-level or ETA-based tracking exists as a proven middle ground in the industry"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's concern should take priority — this is a business strategy decision","Whether regulatory constraints in the operating jurisdiction would prohibit or restrict either approach","Actual driver attrition data attributable to location tracking vs. other factors","Whether Customer Success would accept approximate tracking as meeting their transparency goal"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith and representing real concerns","No executive decision has already been made that one team is unaware of","The company operates in jurisdictions where worker location privacy is regulated"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS sharing with customers at all times. Operations requires zero location sharing with customers, ever. These requirements are mutually exclusive and both teams present them as non-negotiable. This cannot be resolved by engineering — it requires executive arbitration with input from Legal/Compliance.","affected_stakeholders":["Couriers/Drivers","Customers","Customer Success team","Operations team","Legal/Compliance","Executive leadership"],"options":["A: Side with Customer Success — share exact GPS. Risk: accelerated driver attrition, safety liability, possible regulatory violation.","B: Side with Operations — no location sharing. Risk: competitive disadvantage, lower customer satisfaction.","C: Executive-brokered compromise — approximate/zone-level tracking (e.g., 'Your courier is 3 stops away' or 'Arriving in ~8 minutes') that gives customers visibility without exposing exact courier position. This is the industry-standard approach used by UPS, FedEx, and most mature logistics platforms.","D: Courier-consent model — let couriers opt into exact sharing with a compensation incentive, default to approximate tracking. Respects driver autonomy while offering a premium experience."],"recommendation":"Option C (zone-level tracking) is the strong industry-standard recommendation. It satisfies the customer transparency goal without creating driver safety or retention problems. Option D is a secondary enhancement. HOWEVER — I am escalating this, not deciding it. An executive sponsor must own this decision with Legal/Compliance input. Do NOT proceed with implementation until this is resolved."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Contradictory requirements from two stakeholder teams make the spec unimplementable as written","business_rule_broken":"No coherent business rule can be defined when two teams demand opposite behaviors on the same feature"},{"violation":"Legal/Compliance has not been consulted on worker location data sharing","business_rule_broken":"Any business rule defined without legal review may violate privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA, labor law) and expose the company to liability"},{"violation":"Courier physical safety concern has not been formally risk-assessed","business_rule_broken":"Sharing exact GPS coordinates of workers with third parties creates a duty-of-care obligation that has not been evaluated"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is being scoped where customers would see their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands zero location sharing due to driver retention problems. This is a fundamental business policy conflict that cannot be resolved at the engineering level —
